1: mizmôr shîr lĕyôm hashshabbāt — A Psalm. A Song for the Sabbath day. The only psalm with this specific Sabbath title — its placement in the Psalter and its content (the divine work celebrated, the wicked's apparent flourishing addressed, the righteous ultimately vindicated) fit the Sabbath's theological scope: the day that celebrates the goodness of completed creation and anticipates its eschatological fulfillment. ṭôb lĕhôdôt laYHWH — it is good to give thanks to the LORD. Ṭôb (good) — the creation-goodness word of Gen 1, applied to the act of thanksgiving. ûlĕzammēr lĕshimĕkhā ʿelyôn — to sing praises to your name, O Most High. Zāmar (to make music, to sing praise — often with instruments) to ʿelyôn (the Most High — the God who governs all).
2: lĕhaggîd baqqōqer ḥasdekā — to declare your steadfast love each morning. Ḥesed announced in the morning — the morning as the time of covenant renewal and the experience of fresh grace (cf. Lam 3:22-23). wĕʾĕmûnātĕkhā ballêlôt — and your faithfulness through every night. The pairing: ḥesed for the morning (the active covenant love) + ʾĕmûnāh for the night (the reliability that sustains through darkness). The full day covered by the two divine attributes — the Sabbath as the day when both are proclaimed.
3: ʿălê-ʿāśôr wăʿălê-nābel — to the music of the ten-stringed instrument and the lute. ʿĀśôr (ten-stringed instrument) + nābel (lute, harp — a large, pear-shaped stringed instrument). ʿălê higgāyôn bĕkhinnôr — and the rippling of the lyre. Higgāyôn (a murmuring, rippling sound — possibly a specific type of music, or the low melodious sound of the lyre; the word also appears in Ps 9:16 as a musical direction). The three instruments create a full ensemble of praise — string instruments covering different registers of sound.
4: kî śimmahătanî YHWH bĕphāʿŏlekā — you have made me glad, LORD, by what you have done. The personal testimony (śimmahătanî: you made me glad — the same verb as the petition of Ps 90:14-15). Bĕphāʿŏlekā (by your deed/work) — the divine action as the source of human gladness. bĕmaʿăśēh yādêkhā ʾărannēn — I sing for joy over all that your hands have made. Rānan (to shout for joy — the loud, ringing cry of joy) at the divine handiwork. The Sabbath context: the work of YHWH's hands that calls forth joy is the completed creation rested from on the seventh day.
5: māh-gādĕlû maʿăśêkhā YHWH — how great are your works, LORD! The exclamation of wonder at the divine deeds — māh-gādĕlû (how great! — the exclamatory māh). mĕʾōd ʿāmĕqû maḥshĕbōtekhā — how utterly deep are your thoughts! ʿĀmaq (to be deep — the depth that cannot be fathomed from below). Maḥshābāh (thought, plan, design — the divine cognitive activity that produces the observed works). The works are visible; the thoughts behind them are deep beyond human comprehension (cf. Isa 55:8-9; Rom 11:33).
6: ʾîsh-baʿar lōʾ yēdāʿ ûkhĕsîl lōʾ-yābîn ʾet-zōʾt — a senseless person cannot grasp it, and a fool does not understand this. Baʿar (brutish, stupid — one who behaves like an animal, without reason; cf. Ps 73:22). Kĕsîl (fool — the morally/intellectually deficient person who rejects wisdom). The two types of incomprehension: the brutish senselessness that cannot see, and the deliberate foolishness that refuses to understand. What they cannot grasp is elaborated in v7 — the mystery of the wicked's apparent success.
7: biphroaḥ rĕshāʿîm kĕmô ʿēśeb — the wicked may spring up like grass. Pāraḥ (to blossom, spring up — the growth verb applied to the wicked as to grass). The reversal of the righteous-as-tree image (vv12-14): the wicked flourish like grass — quickly and visibly. wayyāṣîṣû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen — and every evildoer may flourish. Yāṣîṣ (to blossom brightly — cf. Ps 90:6 where grass blossoms in the morning). lĕhishshāmĕdām ʿădê-ʿad — but they are headed for eternal ruin. ʿAdê-ʿad (forever, into the furthest future) — the flourishing is temporary, the destruction permanent. The temporal contrast: quick grass-flourishing vs. forever-destruction.
8: wĕʾattāh mārôm lĕʿôlām YHWH — while you, LORD, are exalted on high forever. Mārôm (on high — the elevated place of divine reign) + lĕʿôlām (forever). The contrast with the grass of v7: the wicked flourish briefly and perish eternally; YHWH is exalted eternally. The spatial height (mārôm) of divine governance is the frame within which the apparent flourishing of the wicked is correctly evaluated.
9: kî hinnēh ʾōyĕbêkhā YHWH — yes, your enemies, LORD. The kî-hinnēh (for behold) draws attention dramatically to what follows. kî-hinnēh ʾōyĕbêkhā yōʾbēdû — your enemies will certainly perish. ʾĀbad (to perish, be destroyed) — the enemies of YHWH will perish. yitpārĕdû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen — all who do evil will be scattered. Pārad (niphal: to be scattered, separated) — the dissolution of the apparently cohesive group of evildoers. The repetition of kî hinnēh twice in v9 gives a rhythmic emphasis to the certainty of the outcome.
10: wattārēm kirʾêm qarnî — but you have raised my strength like the horn of a wild ox. Rĕʾēm (the wild ox — the aurochs, Bos primigenius, the immense untameable wild cattle of the ancient Near East; cf. Num 23:22; Deut 33:17; Job 39:9-12). The horn of the wild ox as the image of natural, unconstrained power — not the trained strength of a domestic ox but the raw power of the wild. ballōtî bĕshemen raʿănān — I have been anointed with fresh oil. Raʿănān (fresh, green, lush — the quality of newly pressed oil still full of vitality). The anointing with fresh oil may be literal (festive anointing of Ps 23:5) or metaphorical for divine blessing and refreshment.
11: wattabbēṭ ʿênî bĕshûrāy — my eyes have seen the downfall of my enemies. Shûr (those who watch/lurk against me — from shārar: to be hostile, to watch with hostile intent). The eye-witness to the enemies' downfall — the watching-of-destruction echoed from Ps 91:8. baqāmîm ʿālay mĕrēʿîm tishmaʿnāh ʾoznāy — my ears have heard the ruin of those who rose against me. The double sensory testimony: eyes see and ears hear the fate of those who rose against (qûm ʿālay) the Psalmist.
12: ṣaddîq kattāmār yiphrāḥ — the righteous person flourishes like the palm tree. Tāmār (date palm — Phoenix dactylifera, the tall, stately palm whose fruit comes from near its crown, living for centuries; producing fruit into old age). The contrast with the wicked's grass-flourishing (v7): the wicked are like grass (quick, ephemeral), the righteous are like palm trees (tall, perennial, productive into old age). kĕʾerez ballĕbānôn yiśgeh — and grows tall like a cedar of Lebanon. ʾErez ballĕbānôn (cedar of Lebanon — the great mountain cedar, symbol of grandeur and permanence). Two trees representing the two most majestic forms of botanical life known to ancient Israel.
13: shĕtûlîm bĕbêt YHWH — planted in the house of the LORD. Shātûl (transplanted, planted — the passive participle: these are not self-seeded wild growths but deliberately planted trees). bĕḥaṣrôt ʾĕlōhênû yaphriʾû — they will flourish in the courts of our God. The planting location: bêt YHWH (house of YHWH) + ḥaṣrôt ʾĕlōhênû (courts of our God). The righteous flourish precisely because they are planted in proximity to God — the source of their life. The temple courts as the garden where God's people thrive.
14: ʿôd yĕnûbûn bĕśêbāh — in old age they still produce fruit. Nûb (to bring forth fruit — the productive bearing of the tree). ʾbĕśêbāh (in grey-haired old age — the silvery hair of the aged). The contrast with human transience in Ps 90: here the righteous remain fruitful in old age, like the palm whose fruit-bearing continues for decades. dĕshēnîm wĕraʿănannîm yihyû — they remain vigorous and full of life. Dāshēn (fat, well-nourished, flourishing — the full health that comes from proper nourishment) + raʿănān (fresh, green — the word for the fresh oil of v10). Vitality maintained through old age as the covenant outcome.
15: lĕhaggîd kî-yāshār YHWH — to declare that the LORD is just. The final function of the righteous: they bear witness (higgîd: to declare, make known) to the divine justice. Their own flourishing is itself the testimony. ṣûrî wĕlōʾ-ʿawlātāh bô — my rock, and there is nothing wrong in him. Ṣûr (rock — the bedrock on which the trusting person stands, cf. Deut 32:4). ʿAwlāh (injustice, wrong, perversity) — its negation: lōʾ-ʿawlātāh bô (no injustice in him). The closing confession declares the full vindication of YHWH's justice despite the apparent flourishing of the wicked in v7 — the Sabbath psalm ends with the divine righteousness as the bedrock reality.
